InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 4

Builder Mode —
Where the Money Is

Week 4 turns the full audit lens onto Builder mode — the app's most distinctive and commercially significant feature. Eleven findings are logged (D-39 to D-49), covering everything from compressed controls and hidden integrations to a fully unlabelled transport bar and a proposed full-width stacked layout. Sixteen figures are embedded throughout, drawn directly from raw testing screenshots and visual concepts produced during sessions. Visual representations were developed for the purpose of application development.

WeekWeek 4
Date22–24 June 2026
AuthorRuaraidh
Appinnerseed.garden
Carries forwardD-01 to D-38
StatusIn progress
Builder Mode Evaluation Framework. Week 4 applies a consistent four-part evaluation to each Builder mode finding: what was found, why it matters to the user, what the suggested fix is, and what priority it carries. This produces findings that are immediately actionable for development without requiring academic translation. Where a finding connects back to an existing Week 2 or 3 issue, that connection is made explicit. Visual representations supporting this week's recommendations were developed for application development purposes only.
01

Builder mode — first look and orientation

Builder mode opens with three columns side by side: Ambient Music, Guided Breaths and Guided Voices. The page title reads "Build your session." First impression on arrival, raw:

"To me it looks pretty cool, but again I feel like I need someone to give me a guided tour and I shouldn't really feel that way."

Fig. 1 — Builder mode as it currently stands: three compressed columns, an unlabelled transport bar, and no orientation for a first-time user

Finding D-39 — Builder mode provides no orientation for a first-time user

Arriving in Builder mode drops the user directly into three active pillars with controls already set and no indication of what to do first, in what order, or what the end result of building here will feel like. The experience assumes prior knowledge the user doesn't yet have.

Suggested fix: A single orienting line beneath "Build your session" — something like "Three steps. Take your time. Press play when ready." — removes the uncertainty without adding visual noise.

Fig. 2 — Builder mode on arrival: three active pillars, controls already set, no guidance on where to start or what order to follow

Priority: High

02

Ambient Music — four functions in one small card

"Going back to what the ambient sound bar/section actually is, there are 4 functions inc the Spotify bit going on, and I don't think people need to see all at once, and I think it shouldn't be so damn small. I think these would be better spaced one on top of each other with some width about them, and clearer toggles with little arrows or boxes to show the user what this actually does."
Finding D-40 — "Select ambient music…" reads as a placeholder, not an invitation

The Ambient Music dropdown reads "Select ambient music…" with a trailing ellipsis while the Loop toggle is active and the volume slider is set to 80% — yet no track has been chosen and Total shows 0:00. Controls are active around an empty selection, creating a mismatch: the pillar looks configured when it isn't.

Suggested fix: Make the prompt directive — "Choose a track to begin" — and consider greying out Loop and volume until a track is actually selected.

Priority: Medium

Finding D-41 — Dutch content in the Guided Breaths pillar

"Diepe Neusademhaling" appears as the selected breath pattern in Builder mode. This is the fourth distinct context in which Dutch content has surfaced for an English-language user — alongside the page level (D-03), the Load Session modal (D-23/D-35), and Filter mode (D-46). The pattern is systemic.

Suggested fix: Same recommendation as D-03, D-23, D-35 — one underlying content/language issue to be resolved as a single development ticket rather than treated as isolated occurrences.

Priority: Critical — escalates D-03

Finding D-43 — Spotify integration hidden behind an unlabelled icon

The playlist icon on the Ambient Music pillar header opens a Spotify integration panel containing a curated "Garden Playlist" and a field to paste a personal Spotify playlist URL. Nothing in the interface indicates this is what clicking it will do — the icon reads as a generic playlist or queue symbol.

"I didn't really expect that behind that button and there's no real indication that this would give me Spotify playlists or access to Spotify, in fact I didn't even know that could be a thing, so I'm a little confused."

Spotify integration is a premium-feeling feature that would excite a new user — it expands the musical possibilities of the app and allows personal playlists to become the soundtrack to a breathwork session. Keeping it behind an unlabelled icon means the majority of users will never find it.

Suggested fix: Label the icon explicitly and surface the integration inside the expanded Ambient Music card rather than on the collapsed pillar header.

Fig. 3 — The unlabelled playlist icon on the Ambient Music pillar header — nothing indicates a Spotify integration is behind it

Fig. 4 — The Spotify integration panel revealed: a curated Garden Playlist and a field to paste a personal Spotify playlist URL

Fig. 5 — The favourites and discovery panel: strong UX that surfaces well when found — clean search, filters, track descriptions. The quality of what is hidden makes the discoverability problem more acute.

Priority: High — not broken, but invisible

Finding D-44 — Four functions compressed into one small card

Track selection, loop control, volume and Spotify integration are all competing for the same small horizontal card. Each function is given insufficient space, none carry clear labels beyond their icon, and one is invisible entirely. The result reads as fiddly — close to audio production software rather than a wellness tool.

Suggested fix: Stack the four functions vertically as individually labelled, expandable rows. Each row shows its current state at a glance. Tapping a row expands it for adjustment. This also prepares the layout for mobile significantly better than the current compressed card.

Fig. 6 — The current three-column layout: four functions compressed into each small card, read as audio production software rather than a wellness tool

Priority: High

03

The transport bar — what each icon actually does

The Builder transport bar contains five icons. Each was tapped in turn to establish its function.

Fig. 7 — The library icon opens Filter mode — six filter options, all in Dutch

Finding D-45 — Five unlabelled transport icons performing critical functions

The five transport icons carry no labels and give no visual indication of their function. A first-time user has no basis on which to distinguish a filter from a mode switcher from a session library. Three of the five perform functions central to the entire Builder experience — accessing filters, switching modes, and managing the session — all invisible until discovered by chance.

Suggested fix: Labels beneath each icon at minimum. Given the proposed full-width stacked layout discussed in Section 04, the transport bar itself may need rethinking — these functions deserve proper homes in the interface rather than unlabelled circles at the bottom.

Priority: Critical

Fig. 8 — The transport bar: five icons with no labels and no indication of function — filter, mode switch, stop, share/save all invisible

Finding D-46 — Filter mode is hidden in the transport bar and entirely in Dutch

The library icon opens a filter bar with six options: Intentie, Duur, Gear, Stem, Music, Alleen gratis. All in Dutch. This is Filter mode — deferred since Week 2 — sitting inside the Builder transport bar, reachable only by tapping an unlabelled icon. Even a user who finds it cannot use it without knowing Dutch.

Suggested fix: Bring Filter mode up into the browsing experience where it belongs — visible when selecting tracks or breath patterns — and translate all labels to English. This consolidates D-03, D-23, D-35, D-41 and D-46 into a single development priority: language stability across all content-facing surfaces.

Priority: Critical — escalates D-03, D-23, D-35, D-41

Fig. 9 — The wind icon switches to Session/Preset mode, with no label to indicate this

Fig. 10 — The three-dot menu reveals Share session and Save Session — two of the most important actions in Builder mode

Finding D-47 — Mode switching is duplicated across two disconnected locations

The wind/flow icon in the transport bar switches to Session/Preset mode; the mixer/sliders icon switches back to Builder mode. These are the same mode-switching functions already documented in the sidebar panel (D-21 — Preset/Builder/Creator). There are now two separate, unlabelled, visually disconnected mode toggles with no indication they perform the same function. This deepens the structural concern raised in D-21 considerably.

Suggested fix: Consolidate mode switching to one location — the sidebar panel, where there is space to label it properly. Remove the duplicate from the transport bar or make it visually identical and explicitly connected to the sidebar version.

Priority: High — escalates D-21

Finding D-48 — Share session and Save Session hidden in the three-dot menu

The three-dot overflow menu contains two options: Share session and Save Session. These are among the most important actions in Builder mode — sharing creates word-of-mouth and social proof; saving creates return engagement. Both are buried with no indication of their existence.

"A great function could be better if there was a large box showing the artwork and the selection made, from actually building a session, properly gift wrapped and ready for sharing with a friend."

Suggested fix: Surface Share and Save as visible, labelled actions on the completed session card — not in an overflow menu. See the proposed session share card in Section 05.

Priority: High

04

Guided Voices — current state and gap

Fig. 11 — Guided Voices expanded: a dropdown, a volume slider, and a right-arrow navigation affordance

Fig. 12 — "Select voice…" dropdown — clicking it produces no options

Finding D-49 — Guided Voices: no selectable voices currently available

Guided Voices expands to show a "Select voice…" dropdown and a volume slider. Clicking the dropdown produces no options. It is not yet clear whether this is because no voices have been recorded and logged yet, or whether there is a technical issue with the selector. The volume toggle works correctly in this pillar as it does in the other two.

"When I click on select voice below nothing happens, maybe because there are none yet recorded or logged? Shouldn't we beta in a selection process to continue finishing Builder mode?"

Suggested fix: Until voices are available, the dropdown should either be hidden or replaced with a clear placeholder — "Voices coming soon" or "No voices available yet" — so the user understands this is not a broken control, just an empty one. A route to Creator mode (recording your own voice) could sit here as an interim action.

Priority: Medium — noted, to be revisited once voices are available

Fig. 13 — Guided Voices expanded: the Select voice dropdown is present but produces no selectable options — no voices currently available

05

Proposed layout — full-width stacked Builder

The current three-column compressed layout was the starting point for a full layout rethink. The proposal: move from three columns to a full-width stacked sequence — Music first, Breaths second, Voice third — each pillar occupying the full screen width with numbered steps, clear labels, and consistent four-column function panels across all three.

Fig. 14 — Proposed layout, Step 1: Ambient Music expanded with four clearly labelled functions. Spotify sits as a named, visible feature alongside Track, Loop and Volume

Fig. 15 — Proposed layout, Steps 2 and 3: Guided Breaths and Guided Voice following the same structure, with the session summary and play button at the bottom as the destination

What this proposal resolves

The full-width stacked layout addresses D-39 (no orientation — numbered steps provide a clear sequence), D-43 (Spotify hidden — it sits as a named fourth column in Step 1), D-44 (four functions compressed — each gets its own labelled column), and D-48 (Share/Save buried — the session summary at the bottom makes both visible). It also prepares the Builder interface for mobile significantly better than the current three-column layout, which will be tested as part of the upcoming mobile audit. Each pillar header shows the current selection at a glance, so the user always knows what's configured without expanding each section.

06

Proposed session share card

Following from D-48, the completed Builder session should feel like a finished product — something worth sharing, not a configuration screen with an afterthought export option.

Fig. 16 — Proposed session share card: artwork banner, session title, all three components summarised, Spotify and favourites status confirmed, Share and Save both visible. The session is a product, not just a configuration.

Why this matters beyond UX

A Builder session that looks and feels shareable has a meaningfully different commercial trajectory than one that doesn't. A user who shares a session is acquiring another user at zero cost. A user whose completed session looks polished enough to share has a reason to build more. The completed session card is where the social and commercial potential of Builder mode either gets realised or doesn't — and right now it doesn't, because Share and Save are hidden in a three-dot menu at the bottom of a screen with no visual representation of what was built.

07

Running findings log — D-39 to D-49

RefFindingTypePriorityConnects to

08

Week 4 — still to cover

Mobile testing

The full-width stacked Builder layout proposed in Section 05 is expected to perform significantly better on mobile than the current three-column layout. Mobile testing of both Preset and Builder journeys, plus Filter mode within the Preset browsing flow, to follow once the desktop Builder audit is substantially complete. Timing to be confirmed in the next focus call.

Analytics

Jeroen mentioned an analytics implementation during the Week 3 focus call — details to be recalled and included. The analytics framework for this placement will remain a "what to measure" list tied directly to findings, not a build specification. Key candidate metrics arising from Week 4: do users find and use the Spotify integration (D-43), do they discover Share and Save (D-48), do they complete a Builder session rather than abandoning mid-configuration.

Human testing

Jade Ciao identified as a potential external tester — to be confirmed and cleared with Jeroen, with a small budget to be agreed. The human testing brief will be written once the Builder and mobile audits are complete, so testing has a specific hypothesis to validate rather than being open-ended.

Guided Voices

No voices currently available in the selector (D-49). The Builder audit for this pillar cannot be completed until either voices are available for selection or a decision is made about how to handle the empty state in the interim. To be raised with Jeroen.

Still open from Week 3

D-03 (Critical — Dutch language instability) is now confirmed across five separate surfaces: page level, Load Session modal, Guided Breaths in Builder mode, Filter mode, and the "Find your breath" modal title. This should now be treated as one development ticket covering all five contexts.

D-21 (Threefold consistency) remains the highest-priority structural recommendation. Week 4's D-47 finding confirms that mode-switching duplication compounds rather than resolves the issue raised in Week 2.

Friday 13 June focus call notes still not formally logged.

InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 4 · Ruaraidh · 22–24 June 2026 · Phase 1 Complete innerseed.garden
09

Phase 1 close-out — 27 June 2026

Phase 1 is complete. Four weeks of structured UX audit work on InnerSeed Garden are sealed as of 27 June 2026. This section logs the Phase 1 → Phase 2 transition and the decisions made in the 27 June focus call with Jeroen.
Focus call — 27 June 2026 · Ruaraidh & Jeroen

Phase 1 reviewed. Full desktop audit acknowledged as complete. 49 findings logged across four weeks (D-01 to D-49), plus carry-over W1-01 to W1-05. Builder mode identified as the most commercially significant area of the audit. Spotify integration (D-43) confirmed as the standout commercial discovery — no mainstream wellness competitor offers equivalent user playlist import.

Phase 2 scope agreed. The shift from audit to build. Three primary workstreams confirmed for July onwards: User Testing System, Brand Book, and Digital Marketing Strategy.

Brand expansion discussed. International market ambitions, potential retreat programming, and longer-range phase two concepts. Logged in The Seed Vault.

Founder interview agreed. Ruaraidh to travel to Enschede to conduct a filmed interview with Jeroen in his studio. Footage to serve as cornerstone brand content, a marketing asset at launch, and the origin story of InnerSeed Garden in the founder's own words. Full brief in The Seed Vault and Brand Book.

Analytics. Jeroen's existing implementation to be reviewed in Phase 2 and mapped against audit findings. Key question: are we currently measuring what matters?

Phase 2 — three workstreams

User Testing System. A proprietary, ISD-owned research capability. Structured task scenarios built from audit findings, participant recruitment criteria, data capture and analysis pipeline feeding directly into development. First major deployment: mobile. ISD controls the data — no third-party tools owning the insight.

Brand Book. Visual identity, tone of voice, digital marketing templates and systems. Anchor content: the Jeroen founder interview, shot in Enschede. Brand book development begins Week 5.

Digital Marketing Strategy. Tools and systems to support the app at launch and beyond. Channel strategy, content framework, and campaign templates developed alongside the brand book.

Carried forward into Phase 2

Mobile testing — now the first deployment of the User Testing System, not a standalone audit task.

Guided Voices (D-49) — remains open until voices are available. To be raised with Jeroen.

D-03 (Dutch language instability across five surfaces) — to be raised as a single consolidated development ticket.

Analytics framework — to be reviewed with Jeroen and mapped against findings.

Jade Ciao — potential first human testing participant; to be confirmed and budgeted.

InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 4 · Phase 1 Complete · Sealed 29 Jun 2026 innerseed.garden
